description: Detection specs for PII commonly found in Windows servicing/diagnostic logs (CBS, DISM, SxS). Portable, language-agnostic regex specs: each detector's full match IS the sensitive token (context is excluded with look-around), so a redactor can replace match[0] directly. `safe` describes what the token looks like AFTER redaction — a redactor skips tokens that already match it (idempotent), and a residual-PII scanner treats them as clean (so it flags only un-redacted, real-looking PII).
notes: IPv4 is deliberately ABSENT: in CBS/DISM the dotted-quad shape is overwhelmingly a Windows version (10.0.0.0, 6.4.1.0), so redacting it would corrupt version strings and break AutoExtract patterns for ~zero PII gain. Use the `generic` profile if you truly need IP redaction.

Only USER/domain account SIDs (S-1-5-21-…-RID) are matched. Well-known NT Authority SIDs (S-1-5-18 LocalSystem, S-1-5-19/20 services, S-1-5-32-* built-in groups, S-1-5-80-* service logons, S-1-15-* app containers, etc.) are fixed public identifiers and are left intact — they never match this pattern.

Microsoft WinSxS assembly names (amd64_microsoft-windows-*_31bf3856ad364e35_...) are NOT redacted: they are public component identifiers and are load-bearing for corruption analysis. A separate opt-in detector can target third-party assembly names when needed.

detectors.name: win-username-path
detectors.description: Local account name in a home-directory path: Windows \Users\<name>, macOS /Users/<name>, or Linux /home/<name>. The Unix forms are slightly looser (a URL path like .../home/<x> can match), but the category is opt-in; for support logs (CBS/DISM) the Windows form is the common case. Well-known OS profile folder names (Default, Public, Shared, Guest, Administrator, WDAGUtilityAccount, defaultuser0, …) and All (first segment of \Users\All Users\) are treated as safe — they are public Microsoft/Windows identifiers, not customer identities.

detectors.name: user-sid
detectors.description: User or domain account SID: S-1-5-21-<machine-or-domain>-<RID>. The S-1-5-21 prefix marks the account domain (unique per local SAM or AD forest); the trailing RID identifies the account within that domain. Well-known NT Authority SIDs outside this shape (S-1-5-18 LocalSystem, S-1-5-19 Local Service, S-1-5-20 Network Service, S-1-5-32-* built-in groups, S-1-5-80-* services, S-1-15-* app packages, …) are fixed Microsoft identifiers and are not matched. After redaction, fakes use a zeroed domain triplet (S-1-5-21-0-0-0-<RID>).

detectors.name: unc-host
detectors.description: Hostname in a UNC path \\HOST\share. The lookahead requires a single, NON-doubled trailing backslash so JSON-escaped paths (where every separator is doubled, e.g. C:\\Windows\\CBS) don't match each path segment as a host. Device paths \\?\ and \\.\ are excluded by the leading-letter rule. (host is broad and not strong PII — keep it off by default.)

detectors.name: fqdn-internal
detectors.description: An FQDN ending in a KNOWN-internal suffix (db01.corp.local, host.intranet, printer.home.arpa). This deliberately matches ONLY a small allow-list of non-routable/internal suffixes — local, lan, corp, internal, intranet, intra, localdomain, localnet, private, home(.arpa) — so it can't mistake ordinary dotted filenames (config.json, x.amd64.mfl) or public FQDNs (api.example.com) for hosts. It therefore MISSES internal hosts that use a custom/brand suffix; that under-redaction is the safe trade-off. Not strong PII — keep off by default.
